quiz_questions.question_text: Plan A has a $300/month premium. Its annual premium cost is:
quiz_questions.explanation: $300 multiplied by 12 months equals $3,600 per year in premiums. This is a basic multiplication that helps compare annual insurance costs.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a healthy year with no claims, a $200/month plan saves _____ over a $300/month plan.
quiz_questions.explanation: $300 x 12 = $3,600 versus $200 x 12 = $2,400. Difference = $1,200. In years without claims, the lower-premium plan saves money, but its higher deductible can cost more in years with claims.

quiz_questions.question_text: An HSA-eligible high-deductible health plan (HDHP) must have a minimum individual deductible of _____ in 2024.
quiz_questions.explanation: For 2024, an HDHP must have a deductible of at least $1,600 for individual coverage ($3,200 for family) to qualify for Health Savings Account contributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: HSA funds, unlike FSA funds, roll over:

options.text: Only until age 65
Indefinitely — there is no use-it-or-lose-it rule
Until December 31 of each year
Only for one year

quiz_questions.explanation: HSA funds roll over indefinitely year after year, unlike FSA funds that generally must be used within the plan year. This makes HSAs a powerful long-term savings tool.

quiz_questions.question_text: A family earning $80,000 should have approximately _____ in term life insurance coverage.
quiz_questions.explanation: The general guideline is 10-12 times annual income. $80,000 x 10 = $800,000. This amount would replace the earner's income for approximately 10 years.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year-old can get a $500,000 20-year term life policy for approximately:

options.text: $500/month
$25-40/month
$100/month
$5/month

quiz_questions.explanation: Term life insurance is very affordable for young, healthy individuals. A $500,000 20-year term policy for a 30-year-old typically costs $25-40 per month.

quiz_questions.question_text: Auto insurance with 100/300/100 liability coverage means:

options.text: $100K total coverage for all claims
A $300 deductible
$100K per person bodily injury / $300K per accident total / $100K property damage
100% coverage on everything

quiz_questions.explanation: The three numbers represent: maximum per-person bodily injury ($100K), maximum per-accident total bodily injury ($300K), and maximum property damage ($100K).

quiz_questions.question_text: Flood insurance is _____ included in standard homeowner's policies.

options.text: NOT — a separate policy is required, often through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP)
Only in coastal states
Always
Automatically included at no extra cost

quiz_questions.explanation: Standard homeowner's insurance excludes flood damage. Homeowners in flood-prone areas must purchase separate flood insurance, typically through NFIP or private insurers.

quiz_questions.question_text: Bundling auto and home insurance with the same company typically saves:

options.text: Nothing — companies do not offer multi-policy discounts
Only a few dollars per year
50% or more
10-25% on combined premiums

quiz_questions.explanation: Most insurance companies offer 10-25% multi-policy discounts for bundling auto and home (or renters) coverage. However, it is still worth comparing bundled prices to separate best-price policies.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most common reason insurance claims are denied is:

options.text: Policy exclusions (the event is not covered) or insufficient documentation
The claim amount is too small
Filing too early after the incident
The insurance company is acting in bad faith

quiz_questions.explanation: Claims are most commonly denied because the type of loss is excluded from the policy or the claimant did not provide sufficient documentation. Reading your policy and documenting losses thoroughly prevents most denials.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'HDHP' (High-Deductible Health Plan)?

options.text: A health plan with the highest possible premiums
A health plan with a higher deductible and lower premiums that qualifies for Health Savings Account contributions
A dental insurance plan
A plan that only covers hospital stays

quiz_questions.explanation: An HDHP has a higher deductible (at least $1,600 individual in 2024) and lower premiums. The key benefit is eligibility for HSA contributions, providing triple tax advantages.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage' protect against?

options.text: Mechanical breakdowns of your own vehicle
Tickets and fines from traffic violations
Damage to your car from natural disasters
Injuries and damages caused by a driver who has no insurance or insufficient insurance to cover your losses

quiz_questions.explanation: Uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage pays for your injuries and damages when the at-fault driver has no insurance or not enough to cover your losses. About 13% of drivers are uninsured.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'long-term care insurance'?

options.text: Coverage for extended care needs such as nursing homes, assisted living, and in-home care that can cost $60,000-$100,000+ per year
Medicare supplemental insurance
A type of disability insurance for the elderly
Insurance that covers extended hospital stays only

quiz_questions.explanation: Long-term care insurance covers custodial care that health insurance and Medicare generally do not, including nursing homes ($8,000+/month), assisted living, and in-home aide services.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'replacement cost' mean in a homeowner's or renter's policy?

options.text: The current resale value of the item
The insured value listed on the policy schedule
The original purchase price of the item
The cost to replace the item with a new one of similar kind and quality, without deducting for depreciation

quiz_questions.explanation: Replacement cost coverage pays to replace damaged or lost items at today's prices for new equivalents. This is more generous than actual cash value, which deducts depreciation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'actual cash value' (ACV) mean in insurance?

options.text: The maximum amount an insurance policy will pay
The amount of cash in your bank account
The original purchase price of the item
The replacement cost of an item minus depreciation, reflecting what the item was worth at the time of loss

quiz_questions.explanation: ACV = replacement cost minus depreciation. A 5-year-old laptop that costs $1,200 new might have an ACV of $400. ACV policies pay less than replacement cost policies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'insurance rider' or 'endorsement'?

options.text: An add-on to an existing insurance policy that provides additional coverage or modifies terms for an extra premium
A discount applied to a bundled policy
A person who is covered under another person's insurance policy
The person who delivers insurance documents

quiz_questions.explanation: A rider (or endorsement) adds or modifies coverage beyond the standard policy. Common examples include jewelry riders, home office riders, or identity theft protection endorsements.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'elimination period' mean in disability insurance?

options.text: The maximum duration of benefit payments
The waiting period after becoming disabled before benefit payments begin, typically 30, 60, or 90 days
The time required to cancel a disability policy
The period during which the insurance company reviews your claim

quiz_questions.explanation: The elimination period is the waiting period between the onset of disability and when benefits start. A longer elimination period (90 days vs. 30 days) reduces premiums but requires more savings to bridge the gap.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Health Savings Account' (HSA) mean?

options.text: A tax-advantaged account available to HDHP enrollees that allows pre-tax contributions, tax-free growth, and tax-free withdrawals for qualified medical expenses
A savings account offered by hospitals to pay for medical bills
A flexible spending account that must be used within the year
A government-provided health insurance plan

quiz_questions.explanation: An HSA combines three tax benefits (deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free medical withdrawals) with no use-it-or-lose-it deadline. After age 65, funds can be used for any purpose (taxed like a Traditional IRA if non-medical).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'policy exclusion' mean?

options.text: The process of removing a person from a policy
A special provision that enhances coverage
A type of insurance that excludes certain people from coverage
Specific events, conditions, or circumstances listed in the policy that are not covered by the insurance

quiz_questions.explanation: Policy exclusions define what is NOT covered. Common exclusions include floods and earthquakes in homeowner's policies, and pre-existing conditions in some health policies. Reading exclusions is essential to avoid claim denials.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'claims adjuster' mean?

options.text: A professional who investigates insurance claims, evaluates damage, and determines the amount the insurer should pay
A lawyer who represents you in an insurance dispute
The person who adjusts your premium rate each year
The person who sells you the insurance policy

quiz_questions.explanation: A claims adjuster inspects damage, reviews documentation, and determines the payout amount under the policy terms. You can also hire a public adjuster to represent your interests if you disagree with the insurer's assessment.

quiz_questions.question_text: Jake compares Plan A ($350/month, $1,000 deductible, $5,000 OOP max) and Plan B ($200/month, $3,000 deductible, $8,000 OOP max). In a year with $20,000 in medical bills, which plan costs less total?

options.text: Plan B at $8,800 total
Plan B at $10,400 total
Plan A at $9,200 total
Both cost the same

quiz_questions.explanation: Plan A: $350 x 12 = $4,200 premiums + $5,000 OOP max = $9,200 total. Plan B: $200 x 12 = $2,400 premiums + $8,000 OOP max = $10,400 total. In a high-cost medical year, Plan A's lower OOP max saves $1,200.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple (both age 35) needs $1 million in life insurance. A 20-year term policy costs $45/month and a whole life policy costs $600/month. If they buy term and invest the $555/month difference at 7% annual return for 20 years, approximately how much will the investment grow to?
quiz_questions.explanation: Using the future value of annuity formula: $555/month for 20 years at 7% annual return yields approximately $287,000. This illustrates why 'buy term and invest the difference' often outperforms whole life cash value.

quiz_questions.question_text: Maria has a $4,150 HSA contribution in the 24% bracket. She invests it and it grows to $9,000 over 15 years. She withdraws $9,000 for qualified medical expenses. What is her total tax savings compared to using a taxable brokerage account?

options.text: $4,150 — only the contributed amount is tax-advantaged
Approximately $2,160 — savings on contribution deduction plus tax-free growth and withdrawal
$9,000 — the entire withdrawal is tax savings
$996 — only the contribution deduction

quiz_questions.explanation: Contribution deduction: $4,150 x 0.24 = $996. Tax-free growth on $4,850 gain: $4,850 x 0.24 = $1,164. Total savings = $996 + $1,164 = $2,160. The triple tax advantage compounds over time.

quiz_questions.question_text: A homeowner in a flood zone pays $1,200/year for flood insurance. Their home is worth $300,000 and the annual probability of a significant flood is 2%. Is this insurance cost-effective based on expected value?

options.text: It breaks even exactly
No — the premium far exceeds the expected loss
Yes — the expected annual loss is $6,000 (2% x $300,000), making the $1,200 premium a bargain
Cannot be determined without more data

quiz_questions.explanation: Expected loss = 0.02 x $300,000 = $6,000/year. The $1,200 premium is only 20% of the expected loss, making it an excellent value. Flood insurance in flood zones is strongly advisable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sam earns $95,000 and is comparing disability insurance quotes. Policy A replaces 60% of income with a 30-day elimination period for $180/month. Policy B replaces 60% with a 90-day period for $110/month. If disabled, how much more does Sam pay out of pocket with Policy B during the extra 60-day wait?
quiz_questions.explanation: Monthly income = $95,000 / 12 = $7,917. The extra 60-day wait = 2 months without benefits. Out-of-pocket cost for 2 months of lost income = $7,917 x 2 = $15,833. But the premium savings = $70/month x 12 = $840/year. It would take many years of savings to offset one disability event.

quiz_questions.question_text: A renter has $25,000 in personal belongings and no insurance. A fire destroys everything. Renter's insurance at $22/month with a $500 deductible would have cost how much over 3 years vs. the uninsured loss?

options.text: $7,920 in premiums over 3 years; uninsured loss of $25,000
$264 in premiums over 3 years; uninsured loss of $25,000
$792 in premiums over 3 years; uninsured loss of $25,000
$1,500 in premiums over 3 years; uninsured loss of $25,000

quiz_questions.explanation: 3 years of premiums: $22 x 36 = $792. With insurance, the payout would be $25,000 - $500 deductible = $24,500. The $792 investment would have protected against a $25,000 catastrophic loss.

quiz_questions.question_text: A family is reviewing their insurance annually after having a baby. Which coverage changes should they prioritize?

options.text: Only update the health insurance plan
No changes needed — existing coverage automatically covers new dependents
Add the baby to health insurance, increase life insurance to cover the new dependent, and consider disability insurance if not already in place
Cancel all insurance to save money for baby expenses

quiz_questions.explanation: A new baby triggers multiple insurance updates: adding the child to health coverage (within 30 days for a qualifying life event), increasing life insurance for the additional dependent, and ensuring disability coverage to protect the family's income.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tom pays $4,800/year for auto insurance. He gets quotes and finds he can bundle auto + renters with a new company for $4,000/year total (including $300 renter's insurance). What is his net annual savings, considering he currently has no renter's insurance?

options.text: $800 — plus he gains renter's coverage he did not have before
$500 — after subtracting the renter's premium
$0 — he is paying for additional coverage he did not have
$1,100 — comparing total to total

quiz_questions.explanation: Old cost: $4,800 auto + $0 renters = $4,800. New cost: $4,000 bundled (auto + renters). Savings = $800/year, AND he now has renter's insurance protection that he lacked before.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple owns a $500,000 home with $300,000 in personal assets. They have 100/300/100 auto liability coverage. A serious accident results in a $600,000 lawsuit. How much of their personal assets are at risk without an umbrella policy?

options.text: $600,000 — the full lawsuit amount
$0 — their auto policy covers everything
$100,000 — limited by the per-person cap
$300,000 — the full gap between the $300K policy limit and the $600K judgment

quiz_questions.explanation: Auto liability covers $300,000 per accident. The $600,000 judgment leaves a $300,000 gap that the couple must pay from personal assets. A $1M umbrella policy (costing ~$300/year) would have covered this.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lisa, age 55, is estimating long-term care costs. Nursing home care averages $8,500/month in her area. If she needs 3 years of care, what is the total cost, and how does a long-term care policy paying $6,000/month compare?

options.text: Total cost $306,000; policy covers $216,000, leaving a $90,000 gap
Total cost $204,000; policy covers $144,000
Total cost $306,000; policy covers everything
Total cost $102,000; policy covers everything

quiz_questions.explanation: Total cost = $8,500 x 36 months = $306,000. Policy covers $6,000 x 36 = $216,000. Gap = $306,000 - $216,000 = $90,000. Long-term care policies typically do not cover 100% of costs, so supplemental savings are still needed.
